description: A structured approach to verifying that an analytical procedure is suitable for its intended regulatory and quality control purposes.
principles: Define clear acceptance criteria before testing begins.
Validate parameters sequentially based on method type and regulatory scope.
Document every deviation, raw data point, and calculation transparently.
Assess robustness through controlled deliberate variations of critical parameters.
Prove specificity by demonstrating no interference from matrix or degradants.
Apply statistically sound sample sizes and replication protocols.
Align validation scope strictly with applicable guidelines (ICH, USP, EP).

workflow.purpose: Establish baseline requirements and pass/fail limits before data generation.
guidelines: Specify intended use, regulatory framework, and target analyte.
Select relevant validation parameters based on method classification.
Draft a pre-approval protocol with explicit acceptance criteria.
pitfalls: Vague criteria lead to inconclusive or non-compliant results.
Skipping protocol approval causes costly rework and regulatory queries.

workflow.purpose: Generate empirical data for each validation characteristic under controlled conditions.
guidelines: Run replicates following the predefined protocol strictly.
Record raw data immediately and maintain chain of custody.
Calculate statistics using validated software or approved formulas.
pitfalls: Changing conditions mid-test invalidates data comparability.
Ignoring out-of-specification results biases the validation outcome.

workflow.purpose: Verify method reliability against interferences and minor operational variations.
guidelines: Spike samples with potential interferents, impurities, or degradants.
Deliberately vary critical parameters (pH, flow rate, temperature, time).
Compare results to baseline to quantify impact on performance.
pitfalls: Over-optimizing robustness masks real-world operational variability.
Confusing specificity with sensitivity leads to incomplete risk assessment.

workflow.purpose: Formalize findings, ensure traceability, and secure compliance sign-off.
guidelines: Summarize all data against predefined acceptance criteria.
Include raw datasets, calculations, and statistical justifications.
Obtain independent technical review and authorized sign-off.
pitfalls: Omitting negative results or deviations compromises audit readiness.
Using unverified statistical methods triggers regulatory rejection.

checklist: Protocol approved and signed before testing begins.
All required validation parameters tested per regulatory scope.
Raw data fully traceable, archived, and linked to calculations.
Acceptance criteria explicitly defined and met for each parameter.
Specificity confirmed against matrix components and forced degradants.
Robustness variations documented, evaluated, and justified.
Statistical methods explicitly stated, applied, and validated.
Final report reviewed independently and signed by authorized personnel.

source.book: Method Validation in Pharmaceutical Analysis - A Guide to Best Practice
source.author: Ian Small

vocabulary.definition: Closeness of agreement between the value found and the accepted reference value.
Closeness of agreement between independent test results under stipulated conditions, expressed as standard deviation or relative standard deviation.
Limit of Detection / Limit of Quantitation: lowest concentration reliably detected or quantified with acceptable precision and accuracy.
Measure of a method's capacity to remain unaffected by small, deliberate variations in method parameters.

examples.context: Validating an HPLC assay for a new active pharmaceutical ingredient prior to clinical batch release.
examples.outcome: Method proven specific, precise, and robust, enabling regulatory submission without technical queries.
